summary: Plato, a foundational ancient Greek philosopher and student of Socrates, developed a systematic philosophy encompassing ethics, metaphysics, moral psychology, and epistemology. His thought evolved from recording Socratic refutations and ethical intellectualism to proposing his own mature frameworks, most notably the Theory of Forms, the tripartite model of the soul, and the philosophical method of collection and division.

principles.statement: It is always better to suffer injustice than to commit it.
principles.rationale: Doing injustice harms one's own soul, which is the most precious thing a person possesses.

principles.statement: All wrongdoing is the result of ignorance.
principles.rationale: Everyone inherently desires only what is good; therefore, any bad action is a cognitive error about what is truly good.
principles.source_quote: All wrongdoing is done in ignorance, for everyone desires only what is good

principles.statement: The sensible world is merely an imitation of true reality.
principles.rationale: True reality consists of pure, eternal, and unchanging Forms, which sensible objects only approximate or participate in.
principles.source_quote: the world we know through the senses is only an imitation of the pure, eternal, and unchanging world of the Forms.

principles.statement: Virtue is the only intrinsic good.
principles.rationale: Anything else that is considered good is only good insofar as it serves or is used by virtue.

frameworks.name: Collection and Division
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to define and categorize a concept or kind of thing philosophically.
steps: Collect all instances of a generic category that seem to have common characteristics.
Continue subdividing until they cannot be further subdivided.
frameworks.source_quote: the philosopher collects all of the instances of some generic category that seem to have common characteristics, and then divides them into specific kinds until they cannot be further subdivided.

frameworks.name: The Socratic Method (Elenchus)
frameworks.when_to_apply: When investigating ethical truths and exposing the false conceit of wisdom in others.
steps: Ask questions about a difficult or recalcitrant subject.
Elicit answers and proposed definitions from the interlocutor.
Reduce the interlocutor to confusion and self-contradiction to expose their lack of wisdom.

mental_models.name: The Tripartite Soul
mental_models.description: The soul is divided into three competing parts: the rational (loves truth), the spirited (loves honor/victory), and the appetitive (desires food/drink/sex). Justice occurs when reason rules and each part does its own work without interfering with the others.
mental_models.example: Explaining 'akrasia' (moral weakness) as a state where the appetitive part of the soul overrules reason's judgments.

mental_models.name: The Ladder of Love
mental_models.description: A progression of erotic desire (erôs) that starts with physical attraction but ascends to higher pursuits, culminating in direct cognitive contact with the Form of Beauty itself.
mental_models.example: Channeling the power of love into Platonic philosophy rather than squandering it on physical expressions.

mental_models.name: Theory of Forms (Participation)
mental_models.description: Sensible objects possess their properties because they 'participate in' or 'approximate' perfect, abstract exemplars called Forms.
mental_models.example: Particular sensible equal things—for example, equal sticks or stones—are equal because of their 'participation' or 'sharing' in the character of the Form of Equality.

heuristics: Never return harm for harm or evil for evil.
A proper definition must state what is common to all examples, not merely list instances.
Only virtue is good just by itself; everything else is only good insofar as it serves virtue.

signature_quotes.context: Describing the life chosen by Socrates' victims, which led to their embarrassment during his investigations.
From a funerary inscription attributed to Plato for his Syracusan friend, Dion.
Plato's description of his teacher, Socrates.

anti_patterns.description: Indulging in physical expressions of love.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It distracts people into accepting less than their highest potentials and squanders the real power of love, which should be directed toward Beauty Itself.

anti_patterns.description: Valuing the visual and imitative arts (like poetry).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They merely imitate appearances rather than realities, and they arouse excessive and unnatural emotions and appetites.

anti_patterns.description: Defining a concept by providing a list of examples.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: A mere list of examples never provides an adequate analysis of what the value is; proper definitions must state what is common to all examples.
